# Technical Demo | Anthropic DevRel

Live Demo: [https://claude-codes.replit.app/](https://claude-codes.replit.app/)

GitHub: [https://github.com/mattppal/claude-codes](https://github.com/mattppal/claude-codes)

[![][image1]](https://youtu.be/4ggPGH_-euo)

This project includes:

1. A live demo, hosted on a Marimo notebook and deployed on Replit  
2. A fully executable script, `simple_agent.py`  
3. An interactive and editable notebook, `notebook.py`, run with Marimo  
4. A video walkthrough and teardown

Between exploration, research, learning, and writing, I spent about 3 hours on `simple_agent.py` and this write-up.

Afterwards:

* Conversion to Marimo notebook + hosting  
* Video + editing

## Technical approach and key architectural decisions

![][image2]

My goal was to create the simplest agent I could that was able to create, edit, and test files.

For that reason, I opted for a loop with a tool handler function. For a more complex agent, I'd likely take an object-oriented approach or create a multi-agent system.

Our agent keeps working in a loop as long as `stop_reason == "tool_use"` (meaning Claude wants to call another tool). When `stop_reason` becomes `"end_turn"`, the agent knows the task is complete and stops.

This creates natural error handling—if a tool fails, Claude can try a different approach or call the same tool with different parameters, all within the same conversation turn. I've observed this behavior several times: the agent receives an unexpected result, then tries again with a different tool call.

The architecture is discussed in-depth in the video above.

A theme in this demo is the simplicity that can be achieved with minimal dependencies. The agent operates in a loop where it evaluates tools and uses them until it decides the work is complete.

## Why this demonstration

I work for an agentic coding startup. Everyone hears about agentic coding and it's almost guaranteed that devs working with the Anthropic API have some access to tools like Claude Code.

It's a tool devs use every day—but how many know how it works? (I didn't.) Beyond that, many prescribe "magical" properties to LLMs, even devs and logical thinkers.

That’s normal—it feels like magic. But it can lead down a path of misunderstanding or avoidance: “this must be so complicated, I won’t even start.”

As someone who's technically inclined, I often use my intuition to guide what others might be interested in. I can empathize with developers:

- Devs building with Anthropic are likely using AI agents—some use them every day  
- These tools seem new, cutting-edge, and complicated  
- Users might think they require complex abstractions or weeks to build  
- At their core, they are simple  
- We can hook viewers with this simplicity and provide a complete teardown in 15 minutes

This simplicity brings the "wow" and leads the way for learning and understanding. It's grounding to bring practicality to a technology that feels magical.

## How this helps developers understand Claude's potential

This demo bridges the gap between the tools we use every day and how they can be built with Claude. There's a certain appreciation when you learn about something you use daily.

A common misconception is that an abstraction is a prerequisite to starting a project like this. Here, we demonstrate how anyone with a bit of Python knowledge can build an agent with Anthropic.

There's an audience for the deeply technical, but I think the art of education lies in the simple. Leading with simplicity opens the door for builders to continue exploring on their own.

In the age of vibe coding, frameworks and concepts are more important now than ever. 

## What makes builders want to learn more

First, the "wow" moment of prompting an agent and seeing it edit a file—this is why I stacked the architecture diagram and example at the top of the file.

These are the hooks, which are then backed up by the code teardown.

Using Marimo, it was possible to build in a chat interface that doesn't require cell execution and host the notebook remotely. This reduces the friction to getting started and engaging with the notebook.

Second, I think presentation is everything—this doesn't feel like another notebook, it feels like something special.

It's the attention to detail that draws the reader in and makes this feel unique and precious. That's what drew me to Anthropic's brand and that's why this is a powerful example.

It's what makes posts like [*How to Build an Agent*](https://ampcode.com/how-to-build-an-agent) stand out. Attention to detail in educational assets can't be overlooked.

## How Claude was used in creating this demo

Prompting is all about context management. I often start with detailed instructions, then I think about relevant context I can provide that can assist the agent with its task.

As we've seen with papers & blogs on [*Context Rot*](https://research.trychroma.com/context-rot) and [*Context Engineering*](https://www.dbreunig.com/2025/06/26/how-to-fix-your-context.html), context matters. It's not just that the information is present—the structure must be aligned without conflicting information or distractors. 

This is largely the approach I take in my prompting—it's very similar to providing instructions to a colleague or friend. Good prompting mirrors good human communication: clear instructions (what I want done), relevant context (background info they need), and well-structured information (no conflicting details).

One secret: I’m pretty lazy… I hate tedious work. I used prompts for everything that did not provide creative or differentiated value to this project. I use Claude for the following:

### Ideation

I relied heavily on Claude Code for ideation. I used this to understand what was possible before digging in.

For example, I explored a hiking tool:

*Help me build a very simple agent with access to the following tools:*

*\- web search*

*\- an open streetmap tool for finding trails.*

*I should be able to send a prompt like "find trails within 10mi of san francisco".*

*This requires:*

*\- A geocoding tool to convert a location to coordinates*

*\- An OSM tool for looking up details*

*You should do research on the Overpass API for OSM & the Anthropic SDK for tool use*

[*https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/tool-use/implement-tool-use*](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/tool-use/implement-tool-use)

*We should use the Anthropic SDK and only the SDK. Keep the app hyper-minimalist and use the simplest implementation possible*

As with any prompt: frameworks, context, and architecture are key.

I turned this into an agent that was capable of plotting the GeoJSON. This could be another interesting project.

The main point is that I can start with something that works, then tear it down to understand concepts. This is often how I approach learning.

Through the process of iterating on this project, I was able to understand the API, then present them in a way for others to understand.

### Background research and summarization

I have never built an agent with solely the Anthropic SDK. There are many nuances and intricacies that are not immediately obvious. I have plenty of feedback for Anthropic's docs.

The following prompts were useful in understanding the structure and some talking points:

“[*https://docs.anthropic.com*](https://docs.anthropic.com)

*Do research on the Anthropic documentation. Help me understand best practices for building coding agents. What are some useful resources I could explore to learn more?”*

“[*https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching*](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching)

*Help me understand prompt caching. I'd like to know what is cached and when. Can you provide a sample implementation?”*

“[*https://docs.marimo.io/guides/state/*](https://docs.marimo.io/guides/state/)

*I'm trying to understand Marimo state. How can I automatically update state in code? For example, I want to update a variable in a code block and update the state to match so I can visualize the value changing”*

There were many other similar prompts to Claude for explanation, search, and information retrieval.

One interesting workflow is research → voice. While I'm preparing for work, I'll use Claude to do research on a topic, then pass the output to Elevenlabs Reader for a customized podcast on my commute.

I used this method to learn the basics of Nix Home Manager, as well as learn more about tools used in this project (Marimo).

### Vibe Coding

This app was heavily vibe coded (to start). As discussed in [Ideation](#ideation), vibe coding can be a good tool for learning.

- Once I knew I wanted to build a coding agent, I vibe coded a sample implementation.  
  - The code was an absolute mess and very weird.  
- I then rewrote the structure of the app, once I understood enough about the API to have a reasonable opinion on how it should be written.  
- I find a good pattern for building out more sensible apps is to leave `#TODO`'s throughout the code, then have Claude fill them in.  
- For example, writing pseudo-code or the code's structure, then prompting function by function to fill in the details.

It's this focus of being hyper-specific that often yields the best results in projects that are being showcased externally. I also took inspiration from examples online.

One excellent use of vibe coding is personal scripts / software—for example, most of the product marketing assets I create are 75-80% automated (everything but the human touch).

Using Claude, node, & uv, I maintain a directory of scripts, mostly vibe-coded.

These scripts are often used for one-off tasks, but some of them turn into workflows—for example I have a cron job that transcribes my videos and returns timestamps / descriptions. That contributed to part of this assignment.

Vibe coding can be either helpful or detrimental, depending on how it's used. I find it most important to start from a place of understanding.

### Checks and refactoring

I used Claude for all tedium related to refactoring code and doing final checks. I feel it's important to personally write all content, since my voice is not easily replicated by AI.

I have a series of prompts that I'll chain through @ for writing that gets me most of the way there, but a human touch is still imperative.

I used prompts for the following:

* Formatting: Ensure all headers are sentence case and list items do not end in periods
* Spell checks: Please iterate through all markdown blocks in @notebook and check spelling
* Moving things: Help me adapt `@simple_agent.py` to a Marimo notebook, here is the marimo documentation: [https://docs.marimo.com](https://docs.marimo.com)
* More complex Marimo functionality: We need to implement state handling for our Marimo chat, here are the docs: [https://docs.marimo.io/api/inputs/chat/](https://docs.marimo.io/api/inputs/chat/)
* Small syntax and refactors: Make this a one-liner, use Path instead of f.read()
* Types and docstrings: Fix linter errors, update docstrings

## Future improvements

As with any self-guided projects, I made many assumptions about what would be best for the viewer in style & presentation.

- Using a Marimo notebook rather than a traditional Jupyter setup. This resulted in a more blog-like feel, which is what I went for  
- I wanted to create the most professional, approachable, and polished asset that I could.   
  - For a more traditional cookbook, familiar technology (jupyter) is best. But hey, this is an interview—I had to stand-out somehow 😁  
- Building out the agent loop, which is inherently unfriendly to notebooks  
  - Decomposing our agent into functions makes for a better notebook flow  
  - Further ideation is necessary to explore how a notebook would result in a user-friendly experience for a chat app  
- Opting for a longer, narrative-style video: I prefer longer videos that explicitly walk through concepts  
  - For this project, that required 15:00, which is longer than the recommended 5-10 minutes  
  - I made the intentional choice to include all content, because I thought it important to the educational experience

This is not a complex app—that's the point, but there are a number of interesting areas that could be explored further:

1. Implement more robust stop reason handling, retry logic, and try / except blocks  
2. Implement streaming for more responsive messages  
3. Turn our simple agent into a [*Multi-agent Architecture*](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system) with Opus as an [orchestrator](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook/blob/main/patterns/agents/orchestrator_workers.ipynb) and Haiku for lightweight tasks  
4. Play with [remote code execution](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/tool-use/code-execution-tool) for a sandboxed approach  
5. [Reduce latency](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails/reduce-latency) in our responses  
6. The [evaluation / optimizer](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook/blob/main/patterns/agents/evaluator_optimizer.ipynb) pattern seems interesting as well

Thank you for your time.

*For Anthropic*
*August, 2025*
*Matt Palmer*
